lessons.title: Surface Tension: Causes, Measurement and Applications

content.body: Why does a mosquito walk on water? Why does water form spherical droplets? Why does water rise in a narrow tube inserted in soil? These phenomena all arise from surface tension — the tendency of a liquid surface to behave like a stretched elastic membrane. Surface tension is a direct consequence of intermolecular forces, and it has profound implications for biology, engineering, and everyday life in Uganda.
Origin of Surface Tension: Intermolecular Forces

Molecules in a liquid attract each other through intermolecular forces (van der Waals forces, hydrogen bonds, etc.). A molecule deep inside the liquid is surrounded by neighbours on all sides — the net force on it is zero.

A molecule at the surface, however, has neighbours only below and to the sides — there are very few molecules above (only vapour). It therefore experiences a net downward (inward) force pulling it into the bulk liquid.

This creates a tendency for the surface to contract — to minimise its area. The surface behaves like a stretched elastic skin.

**Surface tension γ (gamma)** is defined as the force per unit length acting along the surface, perpendicular to any line drawn in the surface:

**γ = F / L**

Where:
- F = force (N)
- L = length of the line (m)
- γ = surface tension (N/m or J/m²)

Alternatively, γ = surface energy per unit area (J/m²) — the work done to increase the surface area by 1 m².

Typical values (at 20°C):
| Liquid | γ (N/m) |
|---|---|
| Water | 0.073 |
| Mercury | 0.485 |
| Soapy water | 0.025 |
| Ethanol | 0.022 |
| Oil | 0.030 |

**Why clean water has high surface tension:**
Water molecules form strong hydrogen bonds. Disrupting these to create new surface requires significant energy → high γ.

**Why soap reduces surface tension:**
Soap molecules (surfactants) have a hydrophilic head and hydrophobic tail. They position themselves at the surface with tails pointing outward, disrupting hydrogen bonds between water molecules and reducing γ.
Surface Energy and Work Done to Extend a Surface

Surface tension can also be understood as the energy stored per unit area of surface:

γ = W / ΔA

Where W = work done to create additional surface area ΔA.

**Worked Example 1:** A rectangular wire frame has a moveable wire on one side. A soap film is formed on the frame. The moveable wire has length L = 0.10 m and is pulled a distance x = 0.05 m (creating new area). What force F is needed and how much work is done? (γ_soap = 0.025 N/m)

Note: A soap film has TWO surfaces (front and back). Total length of line = 2L.
F = γ × 2L = 0.025 × 2 × 0.10 = 0.005 N
Work done = F × x = 0.005 × 0.05 = 2.5 × 10⁻⁴ J
Or: W = γ × ΔA = 0.025 × (2 × 0.10 × 0.05) = 0.025 × 0.01 = 2.5 × 10⁻⁴ J ✓

**Shapes of liquid surfaces — why spheres?**
A sphere has the minimum surface area for a given volume. Since surface tension acts to minimise surface area, free liquid drops become spherical (when gravity effects are negligible — small drops).

In Uganda, drops of morning dew on spiderwebs and car bonnets are spherical for this reason.
Excess Pressure in Bubbles and Droplets

Surface tension creates an excess pressure inside curved liquid surfaces.

**Pressure inside a liquid droplet (one surface):**
ΔP = 2γ/r

**Derivation:** Consider a droplet of radius r. If it expands by dr, new surface area = 4π(r+dr)² ≈ 4πr² + 8πr·dr. New surface area created = 8πr·dr. Work done by excess pressure = ΔP × 4πr² × dr. Work done against surface tension = γ × 8πr·dr.

Setting equal: ΔP × 4πr² × dr = γ × 8πr·dr
ΔP = 2γ/r ✓

**Pressure inside a soap bubble (two surfaces — inner and outer):**
ΔP = 4γ/r

(Twice as much because there are two liquid surfaces in a soap bubble.)

**Worked Example 2:** A soap bubble has diameter 4 cm. γ = 0.025 N/m. Find the excess pressure inside the bubble.

r = 0.02 m
ΔP = 4γ/r = 4 × 0.025 / 0.02 = 5 Pa

**Worked Example 3:** Two soap bubbles of radii r₁ = 3 cm and r₂ = 5 cm are connected. Which way does air flow?

P₁ = 4γ/r₁ = 4γ/0.03 = 133γ
P₂ = 4γ/r₂ = 4γ/0.05 = 80γ
P₁ > P₂ → air flows from the smaller bubble into the larger one.
The smaller bubble shrinks and the larger one grows — this is why it is harder to blow up a deflated balloon than to add air to an already-inflated one!
Capillarity and Angle of Contact

**Capillarity** (capillary action) is the rise or depression of a liquid in a narrow tube due to surface tension.

**Angle of contact θ** is the angle between the liquid surface and the solid wall, measured through the liquid.

- **θ < 90° (wetting liquid):** Adhesive forces (liquid-solid) > Cohesive forces (liquid-liquid). Liquid wets the surface, meniscus curves upward → capillary RISE. Example: water in glass.
- **θ > 90° (non-wetting liquid):** Cohesive > Adhesive. Liquid does not wet the surface, meniscus curves downward → capillary DEPRESSION. Example: mercury in glass.

**Formula for capillary rise/fall:**
Height h of liquid column:
**h = 2γ cos θ / (ρgr)**

Where:
- γ = surface tension
- θ = angle of contact
- ρ = density of liquid
- g = gravitational acceleration
- r = radius of tube

**Derivation:**
Upward force (surface tension, acting around circumference of tube): F = 2πr × γ cos θ
Weight of liquid column: W = ρg × πr² × h

For equilibrium: F = W
2πrγ cos θ = ρgπr²h
h = 2γ cos θ / (ρgr) ✓

**Worked Example 4:** Water (γ = 0.073 N/m, θ = 0°, ρ = 1000 kg/m³) rises in a capillary tube of radius 0.5 mm. Find the height of rise. (g = 10 m/s²)

h = 2 × 0.073 × cos 0° / (1000 × 10 × 0.0005)
= 0.146 / 5 = 0.0292 m = 2.92 cm

**Applications of capillarity:**
- Water rises in soil pores and tree xylem vessels (vital for plant nutrition in Uganda's farms)
- Blotting paper absorbs ink by capillary action
- Wicks in oil lamps draw up fuel
- Blood spreads in capillary tubes used for blood tests
- Building dampness: water rises through brick and mortar (damp-proof course prevents this)

content.title: Summary
content.body: Surface tension γ = F/L = surface energy per unit area. Arises from unbalanced intermolecular forces at the liquid surface. Excess pressure: liquid droplet ΔP = 2γ/r; soap bubble ΔP = 4γ/r. Capillary rise h = 2γcosθ/(ρgr). Wetting liquids (θ < 90°) rise; non-wetting (θ > 90°) fall.

content.title: Key Terms
content.body:
• Surface tension (γ): Force per unit length along a liquid surface; γ = F/L; unit: N/m
• Angle of contact (θ): Angle between liquid surface and solid wall measured through the liquid; < 90° for wetting, > 90° for non-wetting
• Capillarity: Rise or depression of a liquid in a narrow tube: h = 2γcosθ/(ρgr)
• Excess pressure: Extra pressure inside a curved liquid surface: ΔP = 2γ/r (droplet) or 4γ/r (soap bubble)

questions.question: Surface tension is defined as:
options: Force per unit area on a liquid surface
Force per unit length along a liquid surface
Energy per unit volume of a liquid
questions.answer: Force per unit length along a liquid surface
questions.explanation: Surface tension γ = F/L; force per unit length acting along the liquid surface (or equivalently, surface energy per unit area). Unit: N/m.

questions.question: Archimedes' principle states that the upthrust on a body immersed in a fluid equals:
options: The pressure at the base of the body times its area
The density of the fluid times the body's volume
questions.explanation: Archimedes' principle: upthrust = weight of fluid displaced = ρ_f × V_displaced × g.

questions.question: Water flows through a pipe that narrows from area A to area A/4. If the speed in the wide section is v, what is the speed in the narrow section?
questions.explanation: Continuity: A₁v₁ = A₂v₂. A×v = (A/4)×v₂. v₂ = 4v.

questions.question: A hydraulic press has pistons of areas 2 cm² and 50 cm². A force of 20 N is applied to the small piston. What force is produced at the large piston?
A soap bubble of radius r has excess pressure ΔP inside it. If the radius doubles to 2r, what is the new excess pressure?
questions.explanation: ΔP = 4γ/r. If r doubles: ΔP_new = 4γ/(2r) = ΔP/2.

questions.question: Water rises 3 cm in a capillary tube of radius 0.5 mm. What would be the rise in a tube of radius 0.25 mm? (all other conditions identical)
questions.explanation: h = 2γcosθ/(ρgr) ∝ 1/r. If r halves, h doubles. h₂ = 2 × 3 = 6 cm.

questions.question: An object weighs 60 N in air and 45 N when fully submerged in a liquid of density 800 kg/m³. What is the volume of the object? (g = 10 m/s²)

lessons.title: Viscosity and its Effect on Motion in Fluids

content.body: When you pour honey and water from the same height, honey flows much more slowly. When a ball-bearing falls through engine oil, it falls much more slowly than in water. This resistance to flow — viscosity — is a fundamental property of fluids that affects everything from blood flow in arteries to industrial pipeline design.
Viscosity: Definition and Newton's Law of Viscosity

**Viscosity** is the property of a fluid that resists relative motion between adjacent layers. It arises from intermolecular forces between fluid layers moving at different speeds.

Consider two parallel plates separated by fluid, with the lower plate stationary and the upper plate moving at velocity v. The fluid velocity varies linearly from 0 (at the lower plate) to v (at the upper plate) — this is called **laminar flow**.

**Newton's Law of Viscosity:**
The shear stress (force per unit area) required to maintain this velocity gradient is:

**τ = η × (dv/dx)**

Where:
- τ = shear stress (Pa or N/m²)
- η = dynamic (absolute) viscosity (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
- dv/dx = velocity gradient (s⁻¹)

So: **F = η × A × (dv/dx)**

**Dimensions of viscosity:** [η] = ML⁻¹T⁻¹
**Units:** Pa·s = kg m⁻¹ s⁻¹ (also called Poiseuille, Pl)

Typical values at 20°C:
| Fluid | η (Pa·s) |
|---|---|
| Air | 1.8 × 10⁻⁵ |
| Water | 1.0 × 10⁻³ |
| Blood | 3–4 × 10⁻³ |
| Honey | ~10 |
| Engine oil (SAE 30) | 0.1–0.3 |

**Effect of temperature on viscosity:**
- Liquids: viscosity DECREASES with increasing temperature (molecules have more energy to overcome intermolecular bonds)
- Gases: viscosity INCREASES with temperature (more molecular collisions between layers)

**Kinematic viscosity ν = η/ρ** (unit: m²/s)
Stokes' Law and Terminal Velocity

When a sphere of radius r moves at velocity v through a viscous fluid of viscosity η, the viscous drag force (Stokes' drag) is:

**F_drag = 6πηrv** (Stokes' Law)

This applies when the flow is laminar (smooth, not turbulent) — satisfied when the Reynolds number Re = ρvd/η < ~1.

**Terminal velocity:**
As a sphere falls through a viscous fluid, three forces act:
1. Weight W = (4/3)πr³ ρ_s g (downward)
2. Upthrust (Archimedes) U = (4/3)πr³ ρ_f g (upward)
3. Stokes' drag F = 6πηrv (upward, opposing motion)

At terminal velocity v_T, acceleration = 0:
W = U + F
(4/3)πr³ ρ_s g = (4/3)πr³ ρ_f g + 6πηr v_T

Solving for v_T:
**v_T = 2r²(ρ_s − ρ_f)g / (9η)**

**Worked Example 1:** A steel ball bearing (r = 1 mm = 0.001 m, ρ_s = 7800 kg/m³) falls through glycerol (η = 1.5 Pa·s, ρ_f = 1260 kg/m³). Find the terminal velocity. (g = 10 m/s²)

v_T = 2 × (0.001)² × (7800 − 1260) × 10 / (9 × 1.5)
= 2 × 10⁻⁶ × 6540 × 10 / 13.5
= 2 × 10⁻⁶ × 65400 / 13.5
= 0.1308/13.5
= 9.69 × 10⁻³ m/s ≈ 9.7 mm/s

**Worked Example 2:** A raindrop of radius 2 mm falls through air (η = 1.8 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s, ρ_air ≈ 1.2 kg/m³). ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³. Find the terminal velocity. (g = 10 m/s²)

v_T = 2 × (0.002)² × (1000 − 1.2) × 10 / (9 × 1.8 × 10⁻⁵)
= 2 × 4×10⁻⁶ × 998.8 × 10 / (1.62×10⁻⁴)
= 7.99×10⁻² / 1.62×10⁻⁴
≈ 493 m/s

This is far too high for Stokes' Law to be valid for a 2 mm raindrop (turbulent flow). Real terminal velocity of raindrops ≈ 6–9 m/s. Stokes' Law applies only for very small droplets (fog droplets, dust particles).

**Measuring viscosity using Stokes' Law:**
1. Measure the terminal velocity of a sphere falling in the fluid (using stopwatch and measured distance)
2. η = 2r²(ρ_s − ρ_f)g / (9v_T)
Poiseuille's Formula for Flow Through Pipes

**Poiseuille's formula** gives the volume flow rate Q of a viscous fluid through a cylindrical pipe:

**Q = πr⁴ΔP / (8ηL)**

Where:
- r = radius of pipe (m)
- ΔP = pressure difference between the ends (Pa)
- η = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- L = length of pipe (m)
- Q = volume flow rate (m³/s)

**Key feature:** Q ∝ r⁴ — flow rate is extremely sensitive to pipe radius!

Halving the radius reduces flow to (½)⁴ = 1/16 of the original.

**Applications in medicine (blood flow):**
In arteries, Poiseuille's formula applies (approximately). Arterial narrowing (atherosclerosis) — even a small reduction in radius drastically reduces blood flow:
- If radius reduces by 10% (r → 0.9r): Q → (0.9)⁴ Q = 0.656Q (35% reduction in flow!)
- If radius reduces by 50% (r → 0.5r): Q → (0.5)⁴ Q = 0.0625Q (94% reduction!)

This explains why partial blockage of coronary arteries (heart attack risk) is so dangerous.

**Worked Example 3:** Blood flows through an artery of radius 2 mm and length 0.1 m. The pressure drop is 100 Pa. η_blood = 3 × 10⁻³ Pa·s. Find the volume flow rate.

Q = π × (0.002)⁴ × 100 / (8 × 3×10⁻³ × 0.1)
= π × 1.6×10⁻¹¹ × 100 / (2.4×10⁻³)
= π × 1.6×10⁻⁹ / 2.4×10⁻³
= π × 6.67×10⁻⁷
= 2.09×10⁻⁶ m³/s = 2.09 mL/s

**Worked Example 4:** A water pipe of radius 1 cm and length 10 m carries water at 2×10⁻⁵ m³/s. η = 10⁻³ Pa·s. Find the pressure drop.

ΔP = 8ηLQ/(πr⁴) = 8×10⁻³×10×2×10⁻⁵/(π×(0.01)⁴)
= 1.6×10⁻⁶/(π×10⁻⁸)
= 1.6×10⁻⁶/(3.14×10⁻⁸)
= 51 Pa

content.title: Summary
content.body: Viscosity η measures fluid resistance to flow; F = ηA(dv/dx). Stokes' drag on sphere: F = 6πηrv. Terminal velocity v_T = 2r²(ρ_s−ρ_f)g/(9η). Poiseuille's law: Q = πr⁴ΔP/(8ηL); Q ∝ r⁴ — small radius changes have huge effects. Liquid viscosity decreases with temperature; gas viscosity increases.

content.title: Key Terms
content.body:
• Viscosity (η): Resistance of a fluid to flow; η = shear stress / velocity gradient; unit: Pa·s
• Stokes' Law: Drag force on a sphere: F = 6πηrv; applies to laminar flow around small spheres
• Terminal velocity: Constant speed reached when weight = upthrust + drag: v_T = 2r²(ρ_s−ρ_f)g/(9η)
• Poiseuille's formula: Flow rate through a pipe: Q = πr⁴ΔP/(8ηL); extremely sensitive to pipe radius

questions.question: In a horizontal pipe, where the fluid velocity is highest, the pressure is:
questions.explanation: Bernoulli's equation (horizontal pipe): P + ½ρv² = constant. Higher v → lower P. This is Bernoulli's principle.

questions.question: A steel ball reaches terminal velocity v_T when falling through oil. If the radius is doubled (and all other quantities remain the same), what is the new terminal velocity?
questions.explanation: v_T = 2r²(ρ_s−ρ_f)g/(9η) ∝ r². If r doubles, v_T increases by 2² = 4. New v_T = 4v_T.

questions.question: A diver is 20 m below the surface of Lake Victoria (ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³, atmospheric pressure = 1.0 × 10⁵ Pa, g = 10 m/s²). What is the total pressure on the diver?
The surface tension of water is 0.073 N/m. What is the excess pressure inside a water droplet of radius 0.5 mm?
questions.explanation: For a droplet (one surface): ΔP = 2γ/r = 2×0.073/0.0005 = 0.146/0.0005 = 292 Pa.

questions.question: Water flows through a horizontal pipe that contracts from radius 4 cm to radius 2 cm. The pressure in the wide section is 5000 Pa and the speed is 1 m/s. What is the pressure in the narrow section? (ρ = 1000 kg/m³)
questions.explanation: Continuity: v₂ = v₁(A₁/A₂) = 1×(4²/2²) = 4 m/s. Bernoulli: P₂ = P₁ + ½ρ(v₁²−v₂²) = 5000 + ½×1000×(1−16) = 5000−7500 = −2500 Pa. (Negative pressure means suction — below absolute zero is physically impossible, so cavitation would occur.)

questions.question: A wooden cube of side 10 cm floats in water with 60% of its volume submerged. What is the density of the wood? (ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³)
questions.explanation: Fraction submerged = ρ_wood/ρ_water. 0.60 = ρ_wood/1000. ρ_wood = 600 kg/m³.

questions.question: Why does a surface molecule of a liquid have higher potential energy than a bulk molecule?
options: Surface molecules move faster than bulk molecules
Surface molecules have neighbours only below/beside — net inward force means work needed to bring them to surface
Surface molecules are heated more by the sun
The surface has less pressure so molecules expand
questions.answer: Surface molecules have neighbours only below/beside — net inward force means work needed to bring them to surface
questions.explanation: A bulk molecule has balanced attractions from all sides (zero net force, low PE). A surface molecule lacks neighbours above — net inward force means energy was needed to bring it there — higher PE. Surface tension arises from minimising this extra energy.

lessons.title: Pressure in Fluids: Pascal's Principle and Archimedes' Principle

content.body: The pressure of a fluid — whether it is the water in a dam, blood in arteries, or air in a tyre — follows precise laws that allow us to design hydraulic systems, understand weather, and explain why ships float. In this lesson we derive and apply the fundamental laws of fluid pressure, including Pascal's principle (the basis of all hydraulic machines) and Archimedes' principle (the basis of floatation).
Pressure in a Static Fluid

**Pressure** P = Force / Area = F/A; unit: pascal (Pa) = N/m²

**Pressure at depth h in a fluid of density ρ:**

Consider a column of fluid of cross-section area A and height h:
Weight of fluid column = ρ × Ah × g
Pressure at the base = Weight/Area = ρgh

If the fluid surface is exposed to atmospheric pressure P₀:
**P = P₀ + ρgh**

**Key properties of fluid pressure:**
1. Pressure increases with depth
2. Pressure is transmitted equally in all directions at a point (Pascal's principle)
3. Pressure is independent of the shape of the container (only depth matters)
4. Pressure at the same depth in connected fluid is equal

**Worked Example 1:** A diver is 15 m below the surface of Lake Victoria (ρ = 1000 kg/m³). P₀ = 1.01 × 10⁵ Pa. Find the total pressure. (g = 10 m/s²)

P = P₀ + ρgh = 101,000 + 1000 × 10 × 15 = 101,000 + 150,000 = 251,000 Pa = 2.51 × 10⁵ Pa

(About 2.5 atmospheres — the diver's body must withstand this pressure.)
Pascal's Principle and Hydraulic Systems

**Pascal's Principle:** When pressure is applied to an enclosed fluid, the pressure is transmitted equally to all parts of the fluid and to the walls of the container.

**Mathematical statement:**
ΔP is the same throughout the fluid.

**The hydraulic press:**
A small force F₁ on a small piston of area A₁ creates pressure P = F₁/A₁.
This pressure is transmitted to a large piston of area A₂:
Force on large piston: F₂ = P × A₂ = F₁ × A₂/A₁

**Mechanical advantage = F₂/F₁ = A₂/A₁**

However, by conservation of energy (no gain without work):
Work input = Work output (for ideal system)
F₁ × d₁ = F₂ × d₂
d₂/d₁ = F₁/F₂ = A₁/A₂ (large piston moves less distance)

**Worked Example 2:** A hydraulic car jack has a small piston of radius 1 cm and a large piston of radius 8 cm. A force of 50 N is applied to the small piston. Find:
(a) The force lifting the car
(b) The mechanical advantage

(a) A₁ = π × (0.01)² = 3.14 × 10⁻⁴ m²
A₂ = π × (0.08)² = 2.01 × 10⁻² m²
F₂ = F₁ × A₂/A₁ = 50 × (2.01×10⁻²)/(3.14×10⁻⁴) = 50 × 64 = 3200 N

(b) MA = F₂/F₁ = 3200/50 = 64

**Applications of Pascal's Principle:**
- Hydraulic car brakes
- Hydraulic car lifts (garage workshops)
- Hydraulic excavators and construction equipment
- Hydraulic presses in sugar factories
- Dentist's chair
- Aircraft hydraulic systems
Archimedes' Principle and Floatation

**Archimedes' Principle:** When a body is wholly or partially immersed in a fluid, it experiences an upthrust (buoyancy force) equal to the weight of the fluid displaced.

**U = ρ_f × V_submerged × g**

Where:
- ρ_f = density of fluid
- V_submerged = volume of fluid displaced
- g = gravitational acceleration

**Derivation:**
Consider a rectangular block of height h, cross-section A, submerged in fluid of density ρ_f.
Pressure at bottom of block: P_bottom = P₀ + ρ_f g(d + h)
Pressure at top of block: P_top = P₀ + ρ_f gd

Net upward force (Upthrust):
U = (P_bottom − P_top) × A = ρ_f g h × A = ρ_f g × (Ah) = ρ_f g × V ✓

**Law of Floatation:**
A floating body displaces its own weight of fluid:
Weight of body = Weight of fluid displaced
W = U → m_body × g = ρ_f × V_submerged × g

**Worked Example 3:** A wooden block of mass 2 kg and volume 3 × 10⁻³ m³ is placed in water (ρ = 1000 kg/m³). (g = 10 m/s²)
(a) Find the upthrust if fully submerged
(b) Does it float or sink?
(c) If it floats, what fraction is submerged?

(a) U_full = ρ_f g V = 1000 × 10 × 3×10⁻³ = 30 N
(b) Weight = mg = 2 × 10 = 20 N. Since U_full (30 N) > Weight (20 N), the block FLOATS.
(c) At equilibrium: ρ_f × V_sub × g = mg
V_sub = mg/(ρ_f g) = 2/1000 = 0.002 m³
Fraction submerged = V_sub/V_total = 0.002/0.003 = 2/3 ≈ 67%

**Worked Example 4:** A metal object weighs 45 N in air and 30 N when fully submerged in water (ρ = 1000 kg/m³). Find:
(a) The upthrust (b) The volume of the object (c) The density of the metal

(a) Upthrust = Weight in air − Weight in water = 45 − 30 = 15 N
(b) U = ρ_f g V: 15 = 1000 × 10 × V → V = 1.5 × 10⁻³ m³
(c) Mass = W/g = 45/10 = 4.5 kg
Density = m/V = 4.5/(1.5×10⁻³) = 3000 kg/m³

content.title: Summary
content.body: Fluid pressure: P = P₀ + ρgh. Pascal's principle: pressure transmitted equally throughout enclosed fluid. Hydraulic machines: MA = A₂/A₁. Archimedes' principle: U = ρ_f × V × g. Floatation: weight = upthrust; fraction submerged = ρ_object/ρ_fluid.

content.title: Key Terms
content.body:
• Pascal's principle: Pressure applied to enclosed fluid is transmitted equally throughout the fluid
• Archimedes' principle: Upthrust = weight of fluid displaced: U = ρ_f V g
• Law of floatation: A floating body displaces its own weight of fluid
• Mechanical advantage (hydraulic): MA = A₂/A₁ = ratio of output force to input force in a hydraulic machine

questions.question: A metal block (mass 500 g, volume 80 cm³) is submerged in water (ρ=1000 kg/m³). What is the tension in the supporting wire? (g=10)
The capillary rise formula h=2γcosθ/(ρgr). Which step is key in its derivation?
options: Upward force = γ×2πr×cosθ = weight of column = ρgπr²h → h=2γcosθ/(ρgr) ✓
questions.answer: Upward force = γ×2πr×cosθ = weight of column = ρgπr²h → h=2γcosθ/(ρgr) ✓
questions.explanation: Surface tension force upward: F=γ×2πr×cosθ. Weight of liquid column: W=ρgπr²h. At equilibrium F=W: h=2γcosθ/(ρgr).

questions.question: Why does a large steel ship float even though steel is denser than water?
options: Steel is lighter than water when shaped into a ship
The ship displaces a volume of water whose weight equals the ship's total weight — ship's average density including hollow interior < water density
Seawater is denser than fresh water making it easier to float
questions.answer: The ship displaces a volume of water whose weight equals the ship's total weight — ship's average density including hollow interior < water density
questions.explanation: The hollow hull displaces a large volume of water. If weight of displaced water = total ship weight, the ship floats (Law of Floatation). The ship's average density (including air-filled hull) < 1000 kg/m³.

questions.question: The Bernoulli equation P + ½ρv² + ρgh = constant is derived from:
options: Newton's 3rd Law applied to fluid layers
Work-energy theorem: work by pressure forces = change in KE + change in PE of fluid element
Archimedes' principle applied to moving fluid
questions.answer: Work-energy theorem: work by pressure forces = change in KE + change in PE of fluid element
questions.explanation: For a fluid element: W_pressure = ΔKE + ΔPE. (P₁−P₂)ΔV = ½ρΔV(v₂²−v₁²) + ρΔVg(h₂−h₁). Dividing by ΔV and rearranging: P₁+½ρv₁²+ρgh₁ = P₂+½ρv₂²+ρgh₂. ✓

questions.question: A steel ball (r=1.5mm, ρ_s=7800 kg/m³) falls 8mm in 0.5s at terminal velocity in glycerol (ρ_f=1260 kg/m³). What is η? (g=10)
Water (ρ=1000 kg/m³) flows horizontally at 0.5 m/s and pressure 4×10⁴ Pa in a 2cm-radius pipe. The pipe rises 3m and narrows to 1cm. What is the new pressure?
Why does water rise in a glass capillary tube but mercury falls (is depressed)?
options: Water is less dense than mercury
Water wets glass (θ < 90°) while mercury does not (θ > 90°)
Water has lower surface tension than mercury
questions.answer: Water wets glass (θ < 90°) while mercury does not (θ > 90°)
questions.explanation: For water-glass: adhesive forces (water-glass) > cohesive forces (water-water), so θ < 90° and the meniscus curves up → water rises. For mercury-glass: cohesive forces (mercury-mercury) > adhesive forces (mercury-glass), so θ > 90° → mercury falls.

lessons.title: Fluid Flow: Continuity Equation and Bernoulli's Principle

content.body: How does a pipe manage to carry the same volume of water per second whether it is wide or narrow? Why does a fast-moving river exert less pressure on its banks than a slow-moving one? Why do aircraft wings generate lift? All these questions are answered by two fundamental principles of fluid dynamics: the equation of continuity and Bernoulli's principle.
Types of Fluid Flow

**Streamline (laminar) flow:** Fluid flows in smooth, parallel layers (streamlines). Adjacent layers do not mix. Velocity is constant at any given point. Occurs at low speeds.

**Turbulent flow:** Flow becomes chaotic above a critical speed. Eddies and vortices form. Much higher energy dissipation. Occurs at high speeds or around sharp obstacles.

**Reynolds number:** Dimensionless parameter that predicts when turbulence occurs:
Re = ρvd/η

Where ρ = fluid density, v = flow speed, d = diameter of pipe, η = viscosity.
- Re < 2000: laminar flow
- Re > 4000: turbulent flow
- 2000 < Re < 4000: transitional

**Streamlines:** Lines showing the direction of fluid flow at each point. In streamline flow, fluid particles move along streamlines. Streamlines cannot cross each other.
Equation of Continuity

For an incompressible fluid (constant density) in steady flow through a pipe:

**The mass flow rate is constant:**
ρ × A₁ × v₁ = ρ × A₂ × v₂

Since ρ is constant for incompressible fluids:

**A₁v₁ = A₂v₂**

This is the **equation of continuity** — the volume flow rate Q = Av is constant throughout the pipe.

**Physical interpretation:** Where the pipe is narrow (small A), the fluid moves faster (large v). Where the pipe is wide (large A), the fluid moves slower (small v). Think of a river: a wide section flows slowly; it narrows at rapids and flows fast.

**Worked Example 1:** Water flows at 2 m/s through a pipe of diameter 4 cm. The pipe narrows to diameter 2 cm. Find the speed in the narrow section.

A₁ = π × (0.02)² = 1.257 × 10⁻³ m²
A₂ = π × (0.01)² = 3.14 × 10⁻⁴ m²

A₁v₁ = A₂v₂:
1.257×10⁻³ × 2 = 3.14×10⁻⁴ × v₂
v₂ = 2.514×10⁻³ / 3.14×10⁻⁴ = 8 m/s

(Speed increases 4× when diameter halves — area decreases by factor 4.)

**Volume flow rate:** Q = Av = 1.257×10⁻³ × 2 = 2.51×10⁻³ m³/s (same in both sections ✓)
Bernoulli's Principle and Equation

**Bernoulli's Principle:** In the steady streamline flow of an ideal (non-viscous, incompressible) fluid, the total mechanical energy per unit volume remains constant along a streamline:

**P + ½ρv² + ρgh = constant**

Where:
- P = static pressure (Pa)
- ½ρv² = dynamic pressure (kinetic energy per unit volume)
- ρgh = hydrostatic pressure (potential energy per unit volume)

**Derivation (energy per unit volume):**
Consider fluid entering a pipe at point 1 (height h₁, pressure P₁, speed v₁, area A₁) and leaving at point 2.

Work done by pressure forces = Change in KE + Change in PE
P₁A₁Δx₁ − P₂A₂Δx₂ = ½m(v₂² − v₁²) + mg(h₂ − h₁)

Since A₁Δx₁ = A₂Δx₂ = ΔV (constant volume element) and m = ρΔV:
(P₁ − P₂)ΔV = ½ρΔV(v₂² − v₁²) + ρΔVg(h₂ − h₁)
P₁ + ½ρv₁² + ρgh₁ = P₂ + ½ρv₂² + ρgh₂ ✓

**Key consequence:** Where fluid flows FASTER (higher v), the pressure is LOWER.

**Worked Example 2:** Water flows horizontally through a pipe. At a wide section (A₁ = 4 cm²), v₁ = 1 m/s and P₁ = 3 × 10⁴ Pa. At a narrow section (A₂ = 1 cm²), find v₂ and P₂. (ρ = 1000 kg/m³)

Continuity: v₂ = v₁ × A₁/A₂ = 1 × 4/1 = 4 m/s

Bernoulli (same height, h₁ = h₂):
P₁ + ½ρv₁² = P₂ + ½ρv₂²
3×10⁴ + ½×1000×1 = P₂ + ½×1000×16
30,000 + 500 = P₂ + 8,000
P₂ = 22,500 Pa = 2.25 × 10⁴ Pa

(Pressure drops significantly where the pipe narrows and fluid speeds up.)

content.title: Summary
content.body: Streamline flow: smooth, parallel layers. Turbulent: chaotic, above Re ≈ 4000. Continuity: A₁v₁ = A₂v₂ (Q = Av = constant). Bernoulli: P + ½ρv² + ρgh = constant. Faster flow → lower pressure.

content.title: Key Terms
content.body:
• Streamline flow: Smooth, ordered fluid flow where particles follow parallel paths; also called laminar flow
• Equation of continuity: A₁v₁ = A₂v₂; volume flow rate is constant for incompressible fluid in steady flow
• Bernoulli's equation: P + ½ρv² + ρgh = constant along a streamline in ideal fluid flow
• Reynolds number: Re = ρvd/η; dimensionless number predicting onset of turbulence

questions.question: An aircraft wing generates lift because:
options: Air pushes the wing up from below
Air flows faster over the top of the wing, creating lower pressure above than below
The wing is inclined at an angle to deflect air downward
The engine thrust acts upward on the wing
questions.answer: Air flows faster over the top of the wing, creating lower pressure above than below
questions.explanation: By Bernoulli's principle, faster airflow over the curved upper surface creates lower pressure above the wing. The pressure difference (higher below, lower above) creates a net upward force — lift.

questions.question: A submarine (mass 5×10⁶ kg, volume 5000 m³) in seawater (ρ=1025 kg/m³). Net force on it? (g=10)
options: 1.25×10⁶ N upward (must flood ballast tanks to dive)
questions.answer: 1.25×10⁶ N upward (must flood ballast tanks to dive)
questions.explanation: U=1025×10×5000=5.125×10⁷ N. W=5×10⁶×10=5×10⁷ N. Net=5.125×10⁷−5×10⁷=1.25×10⁶ N upward. Must flood ballast tanks to submerge.

questions.question: To find density of an irregular object using a spring balance and water, which formula is used?
questions.explanation: Upthrust = W_air − W_water = ρ_water g V. Volume V = (W_air − W_water)/(ρ_water g). Mass = W_air/g. Density = mass/V = ρ_water × W_air/(W_air − W_water).

questions.question: According to Stokes' Law, the drag force on a sphere moving through a viscous fluid is proportional to:
options: The fourth power of the radius
questions.explanation: Stokes' Law: F = 6πηrv. The drag force is proportional to r (radius to the first power), as well as to viscosity η and speed v.

questions.question: Blood pressure decreases at an arterial narrowing because:
options: The blood becomes thinner at the narrowing
By continuity, blood speeds up; by Bernoulli, faster flow has lower pressure
questions.answer: By continuity, blood speeds up; by Bernoulli, faster flow has lower pressure
questions.explanation: Continuity: smaller A → higher v. Bernoulli: higher v → lower P. Low pressure can cause arterial collapse (worsening blockage) and clot formation — explaining the danger of atherosclerosis.

questions.question: What is the excess pressure inside a soap bubble of radius 2 cm? (γ_soap = 0.030 N/m)
questions.explanation: ΔP = 4γ/r = 4×0.030/0.02 = 0.12/0.02 = 6 Pa. (Soap bubble has two surfaces, hence 4γ/r not 2γ/r.)

lessons.title: Applications of Bernoulli's Principle and Viscosity

content.body: Bernoulli's principle and viscous flow underpin some of the most important technologies in the world — from aircraft wings to medical blood-flow analysis, from the carburettor in a car engine to the spray gun used in Uganda's paint industry. In this lesson we apply these principles to real systems.
Applications of Bernoulli's Principle

**1. Venturimeter (flow rate measurement)**
A pipe with a constriction (narrowing). By measuring the pressure difference between wide and narrow sections and applying Bernoulli + continuity:

P₁ + ½ρv₁² = P₂ + ½ρv₂²
A₁v₁ = A₂v₂ → v₁ = A₂v₂/A₁

Solving: v₂ = √(2(P₁−P₂) / ρ(1−A₂²/A₁²))
Flow rate: Q = A₂v₂

**Worked Example 1:** A venturimeter has a pipe diameter of 8 cm narrowing to 4 cm. The pressure difference is 1500 Pa. ρ = 1000 kg/m³. Find the flow rate.

A₁ = π×(0.04)² = 5.03×10⁻³ m²
A₂ = π×(0.02)² = 1.26×10⁻³ m²

1−A₂²/A₁² = 1−(1.26/5.03)² = 1−0.0627 = 0.937

v₂ = √(2×1500/(1000×0.937)) = √(3000/937) = √3.202 = 1.79 m/s

Q = A₂v₂ = 1.26×10⁻³ × 1.79 = 2.25×10⁻³ m³/s

**2. Aerofoil and Lift**
An aircraft wing (aerofoil) is shaped so that air flows faster over the curved upper surface than under the flatter lower surface. By Bernoulli: faster air above → lower pressure above. Net upward force (lift) = (P_below − P_above) × wing area.

**3. Bunsen burner**
Gas flows rapidly through a narrow jet at the bottom of the burner barrel. The high-speed gas creates a low-pressure region that draws in air through the air holes.

**4. Spray gun / insecticide sprayer**
Rapid air flow across the top of a liquid tube creates low pressure, drawing liquid up the tube and dispersing it as a fine spray. Used for pesticide spraying on Uganda's farms and in mosquito control.

**5. Pitot tube (aircraft speed measurement)**
A tube facing into the airflow with a hole facing forward (stagnation pressure P₀ + ½ρv²) and one facing sideways (static pressure P₀). The difference gives speed:
v = √(2(P_stagnation − P_static)/ρ)

**6. Carburettor**
In a petrol engine carburettor, air flows rapidly through a narrow venturi section, creating low pressure. This draws petrol from the fuel jet, mixing it with air in the correct proportion.

**7. Flow over buildings (wind loading)**
Wind blowing over the roof of a building flows faster over the top than at ground level. Lower pressure on top → net upward force on roof. This is why roofs are blown off in strong storms (common in Uganda during thunderstorms). Roof structure must anchor the roof down, not just up.
Measurement of Viscosity and Terminal Velocity

**Measuring terminal velocity (falling sphere method):**

1. Fill a tall graduated cylinder with the viscous fluid.
2. Drop a small sphere (steel ball bearing of known radius r and density ρ_s) into the fluid.
3. After the sphere reaches terminal velocity (constant speed), time it over a measured distance d.
4. v_T = d/t
5. Calculate η: η = 2r²(ρ_s − ρ_f)g/(9v_T)

**Worked Example 2:** A steel ball (r = 0.5 mm, ρ_s = 7800 kg/m³) takes 25 s to fall 0.3 m in oil (ρ_f = 900 kg/m³). Find η. (g = 10 m/s²)

v_T = 0.3/25 = 0.012 m/s
η = 2×(0.5×10⁻³)²×(7800−900)×10 / (9×0.012)
= 2×2.5×10⁻⁷×6900×10 / 0.108
= 3.45×10⁻³ / 0.108
= 0.032 Pa·s

**Effect of temperature on viscosity (practical implications):**
- Engine oil thins when hot → lubricates better but may leak past worn seals
- Cold weather makes oil thick → engine difficult to start; use low-viscosity oil (SAE 5W-30) in cold climates
- Blood viscosity matters: dehydration thickens blood, increasing flow resistance and blood pressure
- In Uganda's warm climate, most liquid viscosities are lower than in colder countries → advantage for flow systems
Stokes' and Poiseuille's Formulas: Derivations and Problem Solving

**Stokes' formula derivation (dimensional analysis approach):**

The drag force F on a sphere depends on η, r, v. Assume F = kηᵃrᵇvᶜ.
[F] = MLT⁻²; [η] = ML⁻¹T⁻¹; [r] = L; [v] = LT⁻¹

MLT⁻² = (ML⁻¹T⁻¹)ᵃ(L)ᵇ(LT⁻¹)ᶜ = Mᵃ L^(-a+b+c) T^(-a-c)

M: 1=a; T: -2=-a-c → c=1; L: 1=-1+b+1 → b=1
F = kηrv where k = 6π (from exact calculation)
∴ **F = 6πηrv** ✓

**Worked Example 3 (Combined Stokes + Archimedes):**
A glass bead (r = 2 mm, ρ_glass = 2500 kg/m³) falls through water (η = 10⁻³ Pa·s, ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³). Find:
(a) Terminal velocity
(b) Stokes drag at terminal velocity
(c) Verify forces balance

(a) v_T = 2r²(ρ_s−ρ_f)g/(9η)
= 2×(2×10⁻³)²×(2500−1000)×10/(9×10⁻³)
= 2×4×10⁻⁶×1500×10/(9×10⁻³)
= 0.12/9×10⁻³ = 0.12/0.009 = 13.3 m/s

(b) F_Stokes = 6πηrv_T = 6π×10⁻³×2×10⁻³×13.3 = 5.03×10⁻⁴ N

(c) Weight = (4/3)πr³ρ_s g = (4/3)π×(2×10⁻³)³×2500×10 = 8.38×10⁻⁴ N
Upthrust = (4/3)πr³ρ_f g = (4/3)π×(2×10⁻³)³×1000×10 = 3.35×10⁻⁴ N
Net downward force = 8.38×10⁻⁴ − 3.35×10⁻⁴ = 5.03×10⁻⁴ N

Stokes drag = 5.03×10⁻⁴ N ✓ Forces balance at terminal velocity.

content.title: Summary
content.body: Bernoulli applications: venturimeter, aerofoil lift, spray guns, Bunsen burner, pitot tube, carburettor. Terminal velocity measured by timing sphere fall → viscosity via Stokes. Poiseuille Q∝r⁴ critical for blood flow and pipe design. All applications use the same core principles.

content.title: Key Terms
content.body:
• Venturimeter: Device using Bernoulli + continuity to measure flow rate from a pressure difference across a constriction
• Lift force (aerofoil): Upward force on a wing due to higher pressure below than above; caused by faster flow over the curved upper surface
• Pitot tube: Device measuring fluid velocity from the difference between stagnation and static pressure
• Spray gun: Uses Bernoulli principle: fast air flow creates low pressure above liquid tube, drawing liquid up and atomising it

questions.question: A venturimeter: wide diameter 6cm, throat 3cm, pressure difference 2000 Pa, ρ=1000 kg/m³. Approximate flow rate?
An artery of radius r carries blood at flow rate Q. If the radius reduces to 0.8r due to arterial plaque (all else equal), what fraction of the original flow rate remains?
questions.explanation: Poiseuille: Q ∝ r⁴. New Q/Old Q = (0.8r)⁴/r⁴ = 0.8⁴ = 0.4096 ≈ 0.41. A 20% reduction in radius reduces flow to 41% — a 59% reduction!

questions.question: An object floats with exactly half its volume above water. What is the relationship between the object's density and water's density?
questions.explanation: Half submerged means V_sub = V/2. Law of floatation: ρ_f × V_sub × g = ρ_object × V × g. ρ_water × V/2 = ρ_object × V. ρ_object = ρ_water/2.

questions.question: Why does a shower curtain get pushed inward when the shower is running?
options: Water from the shower hits the curtain pushing it inward
Fast-moving air inside the shower (Bernoulli) has lower pressure than still air outside — curtain pushed in
The curtain absorbs water and becomes heavy
questions.answer: Fast-moving air inside the shower (Bernoulli) has lower pressure than still air outside — curtain pushed in
questions.explanation: Falling water creates rapid air movement inside the shower. By Bernoulli, fast air → low pressure inside. Higher atmospheric pressure outside pushes curtain inward. Classic Bernoulli application.

questions.question: Two soap bubbles of radii 3 cm and 5 cm are connected by a tube. What happens?
options: Air flows from larger to smaller bubble
Air flows from smaller to larger bubble
No air flows; pressures are equal
questions.answer: Air flows from smaller to larger bubble
questions.explanation: ΔP = 4γ/r. Smaller bubble (r=3cm): ΔP₁ = 4γ/0.03 = 133γ. Larger bubble (r=5cm): ΔP₂ = 4γ/0.05 = 80γ. P₁ > P₂ → air flows from smaller (higher pressure) to larger (lower pressure). Smaller shrinks; larger grows.

questions.question: Hydraulic brakes: driver applies 150 N to master cylinder (area 2cm²). Caliper piston area 12cm². What force on caliper piston?
